title: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns
summary: The checked-in XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan AHA2736. It encodes Kock's 1902 title page and pp. 1-158: three distinct anonymous Middle English adaptations of Benedict's Latin Rule, two distinct anonymous ordination rituals, and Kock's notes, while omitting the introduction and glossaries. Kock transcribed the prose Rule and Lansdowne ritual from Lansdowne MS 378, the metrical Rule and Vespasian ritual from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, and the Caxton abstract from CUL AB.4.64. The metrical text and Vespasian ritual also descend editorially from prior Böddeker and Maskell editions newly collated by Kock; Holthausen and Kölbing are supporting criticism. EEBO-TCP is a collateral composite transcription, not Kock's exact source. Nineteen cached source deliverables preserve the evidence and rights framework.
review_notes: The three Rule texts are separate Middle English works: prose paraphrase, metrical adaptation, and Caxton abstract. Their shared relationship is to Benedict's Latin Rule, not to one Middle English work.
The two rituals are separate works. Kock calls the Vespasian text analogous; different incipits, extents, wording, and witnesses do not establish a recension relation.
Benedict is source author of the Latin Rule only. Middle English adapters and ritual authors remain anonymous.
Caxton is printer only; the Caxton text names no translator. Kock is editor/transcriber/collator/commentator; Furnivall is limited to running-head summaries.
Manuscript scribes remain unknown and are distinct from work creators. Savile and Anstis are only possible Cotton owners.
Michigan, Google, Internet Archive, and Toronto are digitization/delivery providers, not intellectual creators.
No current public whole-object facsimile was found for the BL witnesses or CUL copy; this is dated negative evidence.
Morgan PML 704 is imperfect and collateral. TCP combines multiple microfilm copies and is not the exact CUL AB.4.64 state.
Public-domain findings for modern editions are U.S.-specific; scan derivatives remain unknown without item-specific licenses.
U-M's current No Copyright-United States assertion and the legacy restrictive XML notice remain separate.

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR XML for Kock (1902)

entities.label: Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet
alternate_labels: Son, Herkyn þe commandementis of þe mastir
entities.description: Anonymous northern Middle English prose translation and substantial adaptation for female religious; distinct from the metrical version and Caxton abstract.
notes: The Lansdowne copy is a transcript, not an identified authorial original.

entities.label: Northern Metrical Version of the Rule of St. Benet
alternate_labels: Regula beati benedicti anglicis verbis translata
All þat wyll lely lyue in land
entities.description: Anonymous 2,516-line northern Middle English verse translation and adaptation for women; distinct through form, expansion, compression, omission, and chapter order.

entities.label: Caxton Abstract of the Rule of St. Benet
alternate_labels: A compendious abstracte translate into englysshe out of the holy rule of saynte Benet
entities.description: Anonymous compendious English adaptation printed as the third part of Caxton's Book of Divers Ghostly Matters for religious men and women with little or no Latin.
notes: Its explicit requests prayer for the unnamed translator; Caxton is only securely the printer.

entities.label: Northern Lansdowne Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
alternate_labels: This is þe furme how A Nouice sall be made & resaiuid to religiun
entities.description: Anonymous procedure for receiving a novice and making her profession, immediately following the prose Rule in Lansdowne MS 378.

entities.label: Vespasian Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
alternate_labels: Here bygyns þe manere forto make a nun
The Manner to Make a Nun
entities.description: Anonymous Middle English and Latin-formula procedure following the metrical Rule in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV.
notes: Kock calls it an analogous appendage, not a copy or recension of the Lansdowne ritual.

entities.label: Rule of St Benedict
entities.description: The abstract Latin monastic Rule attributed to Benedict, common source behind the three distinct Middle English adaptations.

entities.label: British Library, Lansdowne MS 378
entities.description: Fifteenth-century vellum witness of the prose Rule and its following ordination ritual.

notes: Kock milestones: prose Rule ff. 1b-42b; ritual ff. 43a-46a.
Red initials and strokes; Kock reports earlier chemical treatment of faded readings.
No named scribe is established; witness copying is not collapsed into adaptation or authorship.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV
entities.description: Composite parent manuscript containing nine cataloged items; the target component is ff. 66-126.

notes: 1981 BL binding; condition recorded intact.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, ff. 66-126
entities.description: First-quarter-fifteenth-century component containing the metrical Rule at ff. 66r-119v and ordination ritual at ff. 120r-124v, with later additions.

date_statements.label: Witness
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century, with later additions

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, ff. 66-126

physical_description.support: Mixed paper and parchment within the parent manuscript
notes: Middle English at ff. 66r-119v; Middle English and Latin at ff. 120r-124v.
The ritual's English is underlined in red; its heading is later.
(?) Henry Savile and John Anstis are possible former owners, not authors, translators, or scribes.
No named medieval scribe is established.

entities.label: William Caxton's Book of Divers Ghostly Matters, about 1491
entities.description: Abstract print-state node for Caxton's three-part Westminster edition. The Rule is part three, 20 leaves at aa-bb8 cc4. The schema lacks an early-print class, so scholarly_edition is qualified here.

bibliographic.citation: The Book of Divers Ghostly Matters (Westminster: William Caxton, about 1491), ISTC ig00301000, STC 3305.
bibliographic.title: The Book of Divers Ghostly Matters

notes: Caxton is securely printer; no reviewed evidence makes him translator or abstractor of the Rule.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, AB.4.64, Kock's source copy
entities.description: The physical incunable copy from which Kock transcribed the Caxton Rule. The lineage schema has no printed-witness type, so manuscript_witness is used as the closest physical-witness class; the public ISTC record lists Cambridge holdings but does not disambiguate this shelfmark.
notes: No exact current public item record or whole-copy facsimile was located.
This is a printed incunable copy, not a manuscript; its schema type records physical witness status only.

entities.label: CERL ISTC record ig00301000
entities.description: Complete public JSON record for the Caxton print state, holdings, references, and related resources.

entities.label: Morgan catalog record for PML 704
entities.description: Complete public catalog page for an imperfect collateral copy; several Rule leaves are absent.

entities.label: EEBO-TCP B11684, Book of Divers Ghostly Matters
entities.description: Complete TEI P5 transcription of the three-part Caxton book from multiple microfilm sets. The Rule is the third grouped text, tcp:159995:130-149; this is collateral, not Kock's exact source.

entities.label: Karl Böddeker's metrical Rule edition (1879)
entities.description: First complete edition of the Cotton metrical Rule, Englische Studien 2, 60-93; reprinted and newly collated by Kock.

editorial_practices.description: Kock expands abbreviations and typographically distinguishes supplies; XML commonly retains them as HI1 sequences.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Sparse manuscript punctuation and Caxton slashes
editorial_practices.description: Kock claims responsibility for northern-text punctuation and retains but supplements or changes Caxton's slashes.

editorial_practices.representation: Emended text with witness readings in notes
editorial_practices.description: Kock reprints earlier editions where applicable, checks witnesses, introduces emendations, and records source readings.
editorial_practices.scope: Especially metrical Rule and Vespasian ritual

editorial_practices.source_feature: Five texts from three source objects
editorial_practices.description: Kock juxtaposes three Rule adaptations and two rituals without making them one medieval work.
U-M includes the five texts and notes but omits introduction ix-xlix and glossaries 159-218.

open_questions.question: Which Latin or intermediary exemplars underlie each Rule adaptation?
next_steps: Compare each with critical Latin recensions and vernacular Rule traditions.
Do not infer one Middle English ancestor from the shared Latin source.

open_questions.question: Do the two rituals share a recoverable textual ancestor beyond analogous institutional function?
next_steps: Collate them with medieval English profession and consecration ordines.
Retain separate-work modeling unless dependence or a recension relationship is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Can public facsimiles or authorized reproductions of the two manuscript witnesses and CUL AB.4.64 be obtained?
next_steps: Request current Cambridge record and reproduction information.
Preserve physical request routes without asserting permanent non-digitization.

open_questions.question: Can any adapter, translator, ritual author, or manuscript scribe be identified more precisely?
next_steps: Consult current hand descriptions, provenance work, and specialist bibliography.
Keep Caxton as printer absent positive translator evidence.
